**Astrophysical fluid dynamics** is a branch of modern [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") which deals with the motion of [fluids](/wiki/Fluid "Fluid") in outer space using [fluid mechanics](/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics"), such as those that make up the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") and other [stars](/wiki/Star "Star").[[1]](#cite_note-aims_and_scope-1) The subject covers the fundamentals of fluid mechanics using various [equations](/wiki/Equation "Equation"), such as [continuity equations](/wiki/Continuity_equation "Continuity equation"), the [NavierвҖ“Stokes equations](/wiki/Navier%E2%80%93Stokes_equations "NavierвҖ“Stokes equations"), and [Euler's equations](/wiki/Euler_equations_(fluid_dynamics) "Euler equations (fluid dynamics)") of collisional fluids.[[2]](#cite_note-steven_shore-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Some of the applications of astrophysical fluid dynamics include dynamics of [stellar systems](/wiki/Stellar_systems "Stellar systems"), [accretion disks](/wiki/Accretion_disks "Accretion disks"), [astrophysical jets](/wiki/Astrophysical_jet "Astrophysical jet"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Newtonian fluids](/wiki/Newtonian_fluid "Newtonian fluid"), and the [fluid dynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics") of [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxies "Galaxies").

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Text_document_with_red_question_mark.svg/40px-Text_document_with_red_question_mark.svg.png)

Astrophysical fluid dynamics applies fluid dynamics and its equations to the movement of the fluids in [space](/wiki/Space "Space"). The applications are different from regular fluid mechanics in that nearly all calculations take place in a [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") with [zero gravity](/wiki/Weightlessness "Weightlessness").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Most of the [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium") is not at rest, but is in supersonic motion due to [supernova explosions](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova"), [stellar winds](/wiki/Stellar_wind "Stellar wind"), radiation fields and a time dependent gravitational field caused by spiral density waves in the [stellar discs](/wiki/Galactic_disc "Galactic disc") of galaxies. Since supersonic motions almost always involve [shock waves](/wiki/Shock_wave "Shock wave"), shock waves must be accounted for in calculations. The galaxy also contains a dynamically significant magnetic field, meaning that the dynamics are governed by the equations of compressible [magnetohydrodynamics](/wiki/Magnetohydrodynamics "Magnetohydrodynamics"). In many cases, the [electrical conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_resistivity_and_conductivity "Electrical resistivity and conductivity") is large enough for the [ideal MHD equations](/wiki/Magnetohydrodynamics#Ideal_MHD_equations "Magnetohydrodynamics") to be a good approximation, but this is not true in star forming regions where the gas density is high and the degree of ionization is low.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Star formation

An example problem is that of star formation. Stars form out of the interstellar medium, with this formation mostly occurring in [giant molecular clouds](/wiki/Molecular_cloud "Molecular cloud") such as the [Rosette Nebula](/wiki/Rosette_Nebula "Rosette Nebula"). An [interstellar cloud](/wiki/Interstellar_cloud "Interstellar cloud") can collapse due to its [self-gravity](/wiki/Self-gravitation "Self-gravitation") if it is large enough; however, in the ordinary interstellar medium this can only happen if the cloud has a mass of several thousands of solar massesвҖ”much larger than that of any [star](/wiki/Star "Star"). Stars may still form, however, from processes that occur if the [magnetic pressure](/wiki/Magnetic_pressure "Magnetic pressure") is much larger than the [thermal pressure](/wiki/Thermal_pressure "Thermal pressure"), which is the case in giant [molecular clouds](/wiki/Molecular_cloud "Molecular cloud"). These processes rely on the interaction of magnetohydrodynamic waves with a thermal instability. A magnetohydrodynamic wave in a medium in which the magnetic pressure is much larger than the thermal pressure can produce dense regions, but they cannot by themselves make the density high enough for self-gravity to act. However, the [gas](/wiki/Gas "Gas") in star forming regions is heated by [cosmic rays](/wiki/Cosmic_ray "Cosmic ray") and is cooled by radiative processes. The net result is that a gas in a [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium") state in which heating balances cooling can exist in three different phases at the same [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure"): a warm phase with a low [density](/wiki/Density "Density"), an unstable phase with intermediate density and a cold [phase](/wiki/Phase_transition "Phase transition") at low [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"). An increase in pressure due to a [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") or a spiral density wave can shift the gas from the warm phase to the unstable phase, with a magnetohydrodynamic wave then being able to produce dense fragments in the cold phase whose self-gravity is strong enough for them to collapse into stars.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Concepts of fluid dynamics

Many regular fluid dynamics equations are used in astrophysical fluid dynamics. Some of these equations are:[[2]](#cite_note-steven_shore-2)

**Conservation of mass**

The [continuity equation](/wiki/Continuity_equation "Continuity equation") is an extension of conservation of mass to fluid flow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Consider a fluid flowing through a fixed volume tank having one inlet and one outlet. If the flow is steady (no accumulation of fluid within the tank), then the rate of fluid flow at entry must be equal to the rate of fluid flow at the exit for mass conservation. 111 K Appendix : Typos and Corrections 115 Bibliography 117 iv CHAPTER A BASIC PRINCIPLES A.1 Introduction Fluid Dynamics concerns itself with the dynamics of liquid, gases and (to some degree) plasmas. Phenomena considered in п¬Ӯuid dynamics are macroscopic.
We describe a п¬Ӯuid as a continuous medium with well-deп¬Ғned macroscopic quantities (e.g. density ПҒ, pressure p...), even though, at a microscopic level, the п¬Ӯuid is composed of particles.
Most of the baryonic matter in the Universe can be treated as a п¬Ӯuid.
Fluid dynamics is thus an extremely important topic within astrophysics.
Astrophysical systems can display extremes of density (both low and high) and temperature beyond those accessible in terrestrial laboratories. In addition, gravity is often a crucial component of the dynamics in astrophysical systems.
Thus the subject of Astrophysical Fluid Dynamics encompasses but signiп¬Ғcantly extends the study of п¬Ӯuids relevant to terrestrial systems and/or engineers.
In the astrophysical context, the liquid state is not very common (examples are high pressure environments of planetary surfaces and interiors), so our focus will be on the gas phase. Key diп¬Җerence is that gases are more compressible than liquids.

## ADS

## Astrophysical fluid dynamics

#### Abstract

> These lecture notes and example problems are based on a course given at the University of Cambridge in Part III of the Mathematical Tripos. Fluid dynamics is involved in a very wide range of astrophysical phenomena, such as the formation and internal dynamics of stars and giant planets, the workings of jets and accretion discs around stars and black holes and the dynamics of the expanding Universe. Effects that can be important in astrophysical fluids include compressibility, self-gravitation and the dynamical influence of the magnetic field that is `frozen in' to a highly conducting plasma. The basic models introduced and applied in this course are Newtonian gas dynamics and magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) for an ideal compressible fluid. The mathematical structure of the governing equations and the associated conservation laws are explored in some detail because of their importance for both analytical and numerical methods of solution, as well as for physical interpretation. Linear and nonlinear waves, including shocks and other discontinuities, are discussed. The spherical blast wave resulting from a supernova, and involving a strong shock, is a classic problem that can be solved analytically. Steady solutions with spherical or axial symmetry reveal the physics of winds and jets from stars and discs. The linearized equations determine the oscillation modes of astrophysical bodies, as well as their stability and their response to tidal forcing.
